content.text: Developer Support

The Azure Developer support plan costs $29 USD per month per subscription and is the entry-level paid tier. It adds business-hours technical support — access to Azure engineers who can answer how-to questions, guide SDK and API usage, and help debug non-production issues. It is the right choice for individual developers and small teams building on Azure before committing to production workloads.

attrs.alt: Developer Support ticket lifecycle — severity levels, queue, and engineer assignment

content.text: What Developer Support Adds Over Basic
Developer Support builds directly on everything in the Basic plan and adds a single critical capability: the ability to open technical support cases with Azure engineers during business hours.
Capability

Developer
Billing and subscription support
Yes
Yes

Yes

Azure Advisor
Yes
Yes
Microsoft Q&A community
Yes
Yes
Technical support cases
No
Yes (business hours)
Severity B response (< 4 hrs)
No

Severity C response (< 8 hrs)
No

Severity Levels Under Developer Support

Developer Support covers two severity levels. Severity A (critical production outage) is not available — Developer is designed for development and test environments, not production systems where downtime has revenue impact.
Severity
Definition
Initial Response
Available Hours
B — Moderate
Service partially impaired; non-production or workaround exists
< 4 hours
Business hours
C — Minimal
How-to questions, general guidance, SDK/API usage questions
< 8 hours
Business hours
Business hours are defined per the support region tied to the subscription — typically 9 AM to 5 PM local time, Monday through Friday, excluding regional public holidays.
Opening a Support Case Programmatically

The Azure Support REST API (Microsoft.Support resource provider) lets you create, update, and query support tickets from scripts. This is useful for automating case creation from monitoring pipelines or integrating with internal ticketing systems.
Creating a Severity C Technical Support Case

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

SUBSCRIPTION_ID = "your-subscription-id"
API_VERSION = "2020-04-01"

def get_problem_classification(service_id: str, token: str) -> list[dict]:
    """List problem classification GUIDs for a given service."""
    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Support"
        f"/services/{service_id}/problemClassifications"
        f"?api-version={API_VERSION}"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json().get("value", [])

def create_technical_support_ticket(
    ticket_name: str,
    title: str,
    description: str,
    service_id: str,              # e.g. "0f4e9025-c5a3-4b1d-9a32-bc84e7a59b00" for App Service
    problem_classification_id: str,
    severity: str = "minimal",    # minimal | moderate
    contact_email: str = "",
) -> dict:
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{SUBSCRIPTION_ID}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        f"?api-version={API_VERSION}"
    )
    body = {
        "properties": {
            "title": title,
            "description": description,
            "severity": severity,
            "serviceId": f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{service_id}",
            "problemClassificationId": (
                f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{service_id}"
                f"/problemClassifications/{problem_classification_id}"
            ),
            "contactDetails": {
                "firstName": "Dev",
                "lastName": "Team",
                "primaryEmailAddress": contact_email,
                "preferredContactMethod": "email",
                "preferredSupportLanguage": "en-US",
                "country": "US",
                "preferredTimeZone": "Pacific Standard Time",
            },
        }
    }
    resp = requests.put(url, json=body, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

# App Service service_id (fixed for Azure App Service)
APP_SERVICE_SVC_ID = "0f4e9025-c5a3-4b1d-9a32-bc84e7a59b00"

ticket = create_technical_support_ticket(
    ticket_name="dev-sdk-question-20260701",
    title="App Service SDK - GetMetricDefinitionsAsync returns 404 for custom metrics",
    description=(
        "Using Azure Monitor SDK v1.1.0, "
        "GetMetricDefinitionsAsync returns HTTP 404 for custom namespace. "
        "Works for platform metrics. Resource is App Service Standard S1."
    ),
    service_id=APP_SERVICE_SVC_ID,
    problem_classification_id="<problem-classification-guid>",
    severity="minimal",
    contact_email="devops@company.com",
)
print(f"Ticket created: {ticket['name']} — {ticket['properties']['status']}")
Listing and Monitoring Existing Tickets

def list_support_tickets(subscription_id: str, status_filter: str = "open") -> list[dict]:
    """Return all support tickets for a subscription, filtered by status."""
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets"
        f"?api-version=2020-04-01&$filter=status eq '{status_filter}'&$top=50"
    )
    tickets = []
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        for t in data.get("value", []):
            p = t["properties"]
            tickets.append({
                "id": t["name"],
                "title": p["title"],
                "severity": p["severity"],
                "status": p["status"],
                "created": p["createdDate"],
                "service": p.get("serviceDisplayName"),
            })
        url = data.get("nextLink")
    return tickets

open_tickets = list_support_tickets(SUBSCRIPTION_ID, status_filter="open")
for t in open_tickets:
    print(f"[{t['severity'].upper()}] {t['id']} — {t['title']} (since {t['created'][:10]})")
Finding the Correct Service and Problem Classification IDs

Every support case requires a service ID and a problem classification ID. These are stable GUIDs registered in the Microsoft.Support resource provider. You can enumerate them via the API to find the right classification for your issue.
Best Practices for Developer Support Cases
Writing an Effective Case Description
Support engineers triage cases blind — they see only what you write. A well-structured description reduces round-trip time from days to hours.

## Issue Summary
[One sentence: what is failing, what is the observed behavior]

## Environment
- Subscription ID: 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000
- Resource: /subscriptions/.../resourceGroups/.../providers/...
- SDK version / API version: azure-mgmt-monitor==6.0.2
- Region: East US 2

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Call X with parameters Y
2. Observe error Z

## Expected Behavior
[What should happen]

## Actual Behavior
[What actually happens — include full error message and stack trace]

## What I have already tried
- Checked NSG rules — allow 443 outbound
- Verified RBAC role: Monitoring Reader on resource
- Tested with az CLI directly — same error

## Relevant Logs / Output
```
[paste error output here]
```
Attaching Diagnostic Data
Attach structured diagnostic data rather than screenshots of terminal output. The support case attachment API accepts JSON, logs, and archive files.

import os, json

def attach_file_to_ticket(
    subscription_id: str,
    ticket_name: str,
    file_path: str,
    token: str,
) -> dict:
    """Attach a file to an existing support ticket."""
    file_name = os.path.basename(file_path)
    with open(file_path, "rb") as f:
        content = f.read()

    # Step 1: Create an upload handle
    url_create = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        f"/files/{file_name}?api-version=2022-09-01-preview"
    )
    body = {"properties": {"chunkSize": len(content), "fileSize": len(content)}}
    resp = requests.put(
        url_create,
        json=body,
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
        timeout=30,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()

    # Step 2: Upload the content
    url_upload = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{ticket_name}"
        f"/files/{file_name}/upload?api-version=2022-09-01-preview"
    )
    import base64 as _b64
    resp2 = requests.post(
        url_upload,
        json={"properties": {"chunkIndex": 0, "content": _b64.b64encode(content).decode()}},
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
        timeout=60,
    )
    resp2.raise_for_status()
    return {"file": file_name, "size_bytes": len(content)}

# Collect diagnostics and attach
diag_path = "/tmp/diag.json"
with open(diag_path, "w") as f:
    json.dump({"subscription": SUBSCRIPTION_ID, "region": "eastus2", "sdk_version": "6.0.2"}, f, indent=2)

result = attach_file_to_ticket(SUBSCRIPTION_ID, "dev-sdk-question-20260701", diag_path, token)
print(f"Attached: {result['file']} ({result['size_bytes']} bytes)")
When to Upgrade from Developer to Standard
Developer Support is intentionally scoped to non-production usage. The moment your workload affects end users, handles real transactions, or has an internal SLA, you should upgrade.
Signal
Action
You have a revenue-generating service on Azure
Upgrade to Standard ($100/mo) — 24x7 Sev A coverage
You are deploying to production within 60 days
Upgrade now — support cases take time to resolve and build context
You need an architecture review before go-live
Professional Direct ($1,000/mo) includes proactive advisory sessions
Your Azure spend exceeds $5,000/month
Standard saves money vs. the risk of unresolved production outages
Regulatory compliance requires vendor support SLA
Standard minimum; Premier for formal SLA commitments
Integrating Support Case Creation into CI/CD

Development teams can wire support case creation directly into their deployment pipelines — for example, automatically opening a Severity C guidance case when a canary deployment detects an unexpected error pattern that does not match known issues.
GitHub Actions Workflow: Auto-Create a Case on Canary Failure

name: Canary Deployment with Support Auto-Case

on:
  workflow_dispatch:

env:
  AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
  SUPPORT_CONTACT_EMAIL: devops@company.com

jobs:
  deploy-canary:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Azure Login (OIDC)
        uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}

      - name: Deploy to canary slot
        run: |
          az webapp deployment slot swap \
            --name my-api --resource-group my-rg \
            --slot staging --target-slot production --action preview

      - name: Smoke test canary
        id: smoke
        run: |
          STATUS=$(curl -o /dev/null -s -w "%{http_code}" https://my-api-staging.azurewebsites.net/health)
          echo "status=$STATUS" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          [ "$STATUS" = "200" ] || echo "SMOKE_FAILED=true" >> $GITHUB_ENV

      - name: Open support case on unexpected error
        if: env.SMOKE_FAILED == 'true'
        run: python3 scripts/open_support_case.py
        env:
          TICKET_TITLE: "Canary smoke test HTTP ${{ steps.smoke.outputs.status }} — deployment ${{ github.sha }}"
          TICKET_DESC: "Canary deployment failed smoke test. HTTP status ${{ steps.smoke.outputs.status }}. SHA: ${{ github.sha }}"
          TICKET_NAME: "canary-fail-${{ github.run_number }}"
Support Case Creation Script (scripts/open_support_case.py)

"""Create a Severity C support case from CI context."""
import os, sys, requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

SUBSCRIPTION_ID = os.environ["AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID"]
CONTACT_EMAIL = os.environ.get("SUPPORT_CONTACT_EMAIL", "devops@company.com")
TICKET_NAME = os.environ.get("TICKET_NAME", "ci-auto-case")
TICKET_TITLE = os.environ.get("TICKET_TITLE", "Auto-generated support case")
TICKET_DESC = os.environ.get("TICKET_DESC", "No description provided.")

# Azure App Service service ID (stable GUID)
APP_SERVICE_SVC_ID = "0f4e9025-c5a3-4b1d-9a32-bc84e7a59b00"
# How-to / general questions classification (enumerate via API to get exact GUID)
HOW_TO_CLASSIFICATION = "ae6af0b9-1726-3b26-b9d5-9b6d2a5d6f0d"

def main():
    cred = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = cred.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{SUBSCRIPTION_ID}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets/{TICKET_NAME}"
        "?api-version=2020-04-01"
    )
    body = {
        "properties": {
            "title": TICKET_TITLE,
            "description": TICKET_DESC,
            "severity": "minimal",
            "serviceId": f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{APP_SERVICE_SVC_ID}",
            "problemClassificationId": (
                f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/{APP_SERVICE_SVC_ID}"
                f"/problemClassifications/{HOW_TO_CLASSIFICATION}"
            ),
            "contactDetails": {
                "firstName": "CI",
                "lastName": "Pipeline",
                "primaryEmailAddress": CONTACT_EMAIL,
                "preferredContactMethod": "email",
                "preferredSupportLanguage": "en-US",
                "country": "US",
                "preferredTimeZone": "UTC",
            },
        }
    }
    resp = requests.put(url, json=body, headers=headers, timeout=30)
    if resp.status_code == 409:
        print(f"Ticket {TICKET_NAME} already exists — skipping.")
        sys.exit(0)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    data = resp.json()
    print(f"Created ticket: {data['name']} (status: {data['properties']['status']})")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Tracking Developer Support ROI

At $29/month, Developer Support pays for itself when it saves even one hour of engineer time per month. The following script queries all closed support tickets for a subscription and calculates average resolution time — a metric you can use to justify the plan cost.

import requests, statistics
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def parse_iso(s: str) -> datetime:
    return datetime.fromisoformat(s.replace("Z", "+00:00"))

def support_plan_roi(subscription_id: str) -> dict:
    """
    Return average time-to-resolution and estimated engineer-hours saved
    for closed support tickets in the past 90 days.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.Support/supportTickets"
        f"?api-version=2020-04-01&$filter=status eq 'closed'&$top=100"
    )
    tickets = []
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        for t in data.get("value", []):
            p = t["properties"]
            created = parse_iso(p["createdDate"])
            modified = parse_iso(p["modifiedDate"])
            hours = (modified - created).total_seconds() / 3600
            tickets.append({
                "id": t["name"],
                "severity": p["severity"],
                "resolution_hours": round(hours, 1),
            })
        url = data.get("nextLink")

    if not tickets:
        return {"tickets": 0, "avg_hours": 0, "median_hours": 0}

    hours = [t["resolution_hours"] for t in tickets]
    return {
        "tickets_closed": len(tickets),
        "avg_resolution_hours": round(statistics.mean(hours), 1),
        "median_resolution_hours": round(statistics.median(hours), 1),
        "estimated_eng_hours_saved": len(tickets) * 3,  # assume 3 hrs self-research otherwise
        "plan_cost_usd": 29,
    }

roi = support_plan_roi(SUBSCRIPTION_ID)
print(f"Closed tickets: {roi['tickets_closed']}")
print(f"Avg resolution: {roi['avg_resolution_hours']} hours")
print(f"Estimated engineer hours saved: {roi['estimated_eng_hours_saved']}")
print(f"Plan cost: ${roi['plan_cost_usd']}/mo")
Common Developer Support Scenarios
Scenario
What to Include in the Case
Expected Resolution
SDK returns unexpected 404 on valid resource
SDK version, resource ID, full stack trace, equivalent az CLI command result
< 8 hrs — classification or API version guidance
Terraform provider error on resource creation
Provider version, plan output, error message, resource config (sanitised)
< 8 hrs — provider bug vs. config issue
CORS errors on App Service even after config
Browser console error, App Service CORS settings screenshot, request headers
< 8 hrs — App Service CORS override vs. framework
Managed Identity token request fails with 400
Resource ID, scope requested, error JSON, role assignments on identity
< 8 hrs — identity federation or scope issue
Logic App connector authentication failure
Connector type, OAuth flow, error code, trigger/action screenshot
< 8 hrs — connector refresh token or permissions
Function App cold start > 30 seconds on Consumption plan
Function runtime, language, dependencies, trigger type, memory settings
< 8 hrs — optimization guidance or plan upgrade
Operational Considerations
One Developer Support plan per subscription — if you have 10 dev subscriptions, each needs its own $29/mo plan.
Cases opened outside business hours are queued and assigned at the start of the next business day; the response SLA timer starts when a support engineer acknowledges the case.
Developer Support is not available for sovereign cloud subscriptions (Azure Government, Azure China) — check region-specific plan availability.
Escalating a Severity C case to Severity B is possible if the impact worsens; contact the assigned engineer to request re-severity.
Unused cases do not roll over — Developer Support is a flat monthly fee, not a credit-per-case model.
Summary

Azure Developer Support at $29/month per subscription adds business-hours technical support to the free Basic plan — enabling SDK guidance, how-to questions, and non-production debugging via Severity B (< 4 hr) and C (< 8 hr) response channels.
Technical cases are handled during business hours only — not suitable for production incidents requiring 24x7 response.
Use the Microsoft.Support REST API to create and track tickets programmatically from CI/CD or monitoring pipelines.
Each case requires a service ID and problem classification GUID — enumerate these from the Support services API.
Write structured descriptions: environment, reproduction steps, expected vs. actual behavior, and attach diagnostic JSON rather than screenshots.
Upgrade to Standard ($100/mo) at the first sign of a production workload — Severity A response is not available on Developer.
